lenses.guidance: v 13's 'fear not, stand firm' inverts to v 15's 'why do you cry?... go forward'. The structure pivots on the divide: human paralysis (vv 10-12), divine pronouncement (vv 13-14), divine command (v 15), miracle (v 21).

1 Cor 10:1-2 reads the Red Sea (v 21) as baptism — 'all were baptised into Moses in the cloud and in the sea'. The pattern: emerging through water as a delivered people prefigures Christian baptism's shape.

At the chapter's end Israel 'feared the LORD and believed' (v 31) — fear and faith as the redemptive shape of the moment. The covenant promise of Ex 6:6-7 was spoken; here it becomes the people's own confession.

Hebrews 11:29 names the Red Sea crossing (v 21) 'by faith' — the impassable made passable. The pattern prefigures the cross: Christ as the way through what cannot be crossed by human strength, by water then blood.
The Red Sea crossing (v 21) echoes through Pss 77, 78, 106, 114 and Isa 43:16's 'who makes a way in the sea'. The crossing becomes Israel's standard image of divine rescue across Scripture.

'Why do you cry to me? Tell the people to go forward' (v 15) — God interrupts Moses' prayer with command. Pray, then move; trust may need to step into the water before the water steps aside. Faith is verb.
